title: Geometric Prototype Learning in Quantum Hilbert Space with Matrix Product States
authors: Kun Zhang, Lei Ding, Sheng-Chen Bai, Jing Sun, An-Qi Jing, Min Tang, Shi-Ju Ran
abstract: Quantum probability provides a novel framework for formulating machine-learning (ML) problems in Hilbert space. We introduce a prototype-based learning scheme where class representatives are encoded as generative matrix product states (MPS). Because these prototypes reside in the same Hilbert space as quantum-encoded data samples, various ML tasks such as classification and clustering can be performed through geometric measures of quantum states. This approach lifts prototype learning from classical feature space to quantum Hilbert space. Benchmarks on Fashion-MNIST and a real-world electrocardiogram dataset demonstrate that our method outperforms classical prototype approaches while remaining competitive with standard black-box neural networks. We also identify an ``attraction'' effect induced by the quantum-probabilistic prototypes and introduce a dimensionality-reduction scheme based on prototype distances. Our results establish quantum states as an explainable framework for prototype learning, opening new directions for designing ML algorithms in quantum Hilbert space.
summary: This paper introduces a machine learning framework that performs prototype learning directly within a quantum Hilbert space using Matrix Product States. By treating class representatives as quantum states, the method enables classification and anomaly detection through geometric measures like fidelity, offering a more interpretable alternative to black-box neural networks.
main_problem: The lack of interpretability in black-box deep neural networks and the need for a machine learning framework that utilizes the geometric structure of Hilbert space for transparent decision-making.
main_result: The proposed Quantum-Probabilistic Prototype Learning (QPPL) framework outperforms classical prototype methods and is competitive with deep neural networks in tasks like ECG classification and Fashion-MNIST, while providing an explainable, geometric approach to classification and anomaly detection.
method: Classical data is mapped into quantum product states via a feature map, and class representatives are learned as generative Matrix Product States (MPS) by minimizing the Negative Logarithmic Fidelity (NLF) between samples and prototypes.
model_or_system: The framework uses a quantum Hilbert space where data samples are represented as product states of $M$ spins and class prototypes are encoded as Matrix Product States (MPS) with a specific bond dimension $\chi$.
key_observables: Negative Logarithmic Fidelity (NLF), Accuracy, AUROC, Silhouette Coefficient, and Davies-Bouldin index.
important_parameters: Bond dimension ($\chi$), local dimension ($d$), and the rotation parameter ($	heta$) in the quantum feature map.
main_assumptions: The method assumes that classical data can be effectively mapped into a quantum Hilbert space via a predefined feature map and that the NLF metric is suitable for avoiding exponential fidelity decay.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares Euclidean distance in classical space to NLF distance in quantum Hilbert space, demonstrating superior cluster separation and the effectiveness of the MPS-based metric.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of NN opacity, proposes the QPPL framework using MPS, details the feature mapping and NLF distance metric, presents benchmarks on ECG and Fashion-MNIST datasets, and discusses dimensionality reduction and clustering results.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Comparative visualization of clustering structures. (a) Euclidean distance in the classical feature space. (b)–(c) NLF distance in the quantum Hilbert space. (d) MPS metric in the probability feature space.
